events.title: Ethiopian Forces Drive Islamic Courts Union from Mogadishu
events.detail: Ethiopian National Defence Force, backed by the Transitional Federal Government (TFG), swept into Mogadishu on December 28, 2006, routing the Islamic Courts Union (ICU) that had controlled the capital since June 2006. The ICU's militant wing—soon to coalesce as Al-Shabaab ('The Youth')—retreated to the south and began an insurgency. This marks the conventional start date of the Al-Shabaab insurgency tracked here.

events.title: UN Security Council Authorizes AMISOM
events.detail: UNSC Resolution 1744 authorized the African Union Mission in Somalia (AMISOM) with an initial mandate of six months. Uganda became the first contributing country, deploying 1,700 troops in March 2007. AMISOM would grow to nearly 22,000 troops at its peak and operate for 15 years before transitioning to ATMIS in 2022.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Launches Guerrilla Insurgency
events.detail: Under the leadership of Aden Hashi Ayro and later Ahmed Abdi Godane, Al-Shabaab launched a sustained insurgency across south and central Somalia. The group adopted IED tactics, targeted assassinations, and ambushes against TFG and Ethiopian forces. By mid-2007, fighting in Mogadishu had killed hundreds of civilians and displaced 340,000 people.

events.title: US Airstrike Kills Al-Shabaab Commander Aden Hashi Ayro
events.detail: A US Navy cruise missile strike on Dusa Marreb, Galgaduud region, killed Al-Shabaab co-founder and military commander Aden Hashi Ayro and approximately 10 associates on May 1, 2008. Ahmed Abdi Godane (Abu Zubayr) assumed leadership. Rather than crippling the group, the strike accelerated Godane's centralization of power and Al-Shabaab's radicalization.

events.title: Ethiopian Forces Withdraw from Somalia
events.detail: Under domestic political pressure and international criticism, Ethiopian forces completed their withdrawal from Somalia in January 2009, handing security to a weakened AMISOM and TFG. Al-Shabaab rapidly advanced, capturing much of south/central Somalia. Within months, the group controlled more than half of the country including most of Mogadishu outside the AMISOM perimeter.

events.title: Maersk Alabama Hijacking — US Captain Held Hostage
events.detail: Somali pirates hijacked the US-flagged container ship MV Maersk Alabama on April 8, 2009—the first US-flagged vessel seized in nearly 200 years. Captain Richard Phillips was held hostage on a lifeboat for four days before US Navy SEAL snipers simultaneously killed three pirates and freed Phillips on April 12. The incident brought global attention to Somali piracy at its peak, and inspired the 2013 film 'Captain Phillips.'

events.title: Al-Shabaab Formally Aligns with Al-Qaeda
events.detail: Al-Shabaab leader Ahmed Abdi Godane declared formal alliance with Al-Qaeda in a video statement in February 2010. Al-Qaeda officially accepted Al-Shabaab's allegiance in February 2012, designating it a regional affiliate. This linkage brought international resources and expertise but also increased Western counter-terrorism focus on the group.

events.title: Kampala Bombings Kill 74 World Cup Viewers
events.detail: Al-Shabaab carried out coordinated suicide bombings in Kampala, Uganda on July 11, 2010, targeting crowds watching the World Cup final at a rugby club and Ethiopian Village restaurant. 74 people were killed and 85 injured in retaliation for Uganda's AMISOM contribution. The attack marked Al-Shabaab's first major external strike and demonstrated its transnational reach.

events.title: AMISOM Surge Takes Strategic Mogadishu Neighborhoods
events.detail: With AMISOM reinforced to 9,000 troops and backed by increased US support, AMISOM and TFG forces launched a major offensive in Mogadishu beginning in early 2011. By mid-year, AMISOM had taken control of key districts including Bakara Market—Al-Shabaab's main revenue hub—and Hamarweyne. Al-Shabaab fighters were pushed to the outskirts.

events.title: UN Declares Famine in Southern Somalia
events.detail: On July 20, 2011, the UN officially declared famine in the Lower Shabelle and Bakool regions of southern Somalia—the first famine declaration anywhere in the world since Ethiopia in 1984. The crisis was caused by the worst drought in 60 years combined with Al-Shabaab's ban on foreign aid agencies and conflict-driven displacement. An estimated 258,000 people died, half of them children under 5. Over 750,000 faced imminent risk of starvation.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Withdraws from Mogadishu
events.detail: On August 6, 2011, Al-Shabaab announced it was withdrawing from Mogadishu, framing it as a 'tactical retreat' rather than defeat. AMISOM and TFG forces took full control of the capital for the first time in two decades. The withdrawal freed up humanitarian access and marked a major turning point, though Al-Shabaab retained ability to launch deadly attacks in the city via VBIED and suicide bombers.

events.title: Federal Government of Somalia Formally Established
events.detail: On September 10, 2012, Hassan Sheikh Mohamud was elected as the first president of the Federal Government of Somalia (FGS), ending the Transitional Federal Government period. Somalia gained its first permanent government in over two decades. The UN arms embargo was partially lifted to allow weapons for the new government's forces.

events.title: Allied Forces Capture Strategic Port of Kismayo
events.detail: Kenyan Defence Forces (KDF) and Ras Kamboni Brigade (Somali clan militia) captured Kismayo on October 5, 2012, denying Al-Shabaab its most important economic hub. The group had earned an estimated $25–50 million annually from Kismayo's port through charcoal exports and taxation. The loss forced Al-Shabaab to restructure its revenue model toward inland taxation.

events.title: Westgate Shopping Mall Attack, Nairobi — 67 Killed
events.detail: On September 21, 2013, Al-Shabaab gunmen stormed the upscale Westgate shopping center in Nairobi, Kenya, killing at least 67 people and wounding 175 in a 4-day siege. The attack demonstrated Al-Shabaab's continued capability for complex external operations despite territorial losses, and was explicitly framed as retaliation for Kenya's military presence in Somalia. Western nationals including citizens of France, UK, Canada, and China were among the dead.

events.title: US Airstrike Kills Al-Shabaab Emir Ahmed Abdi Godane
events.detail: US F-15 jets and Reaper drones struck an Al-Shabaab leadership camp in the Lower Shabelle region on September 1, 2014, killing Ahmed Abdi Godane—the group's most powerful and ideologically extreme leader since its founding. Ahmad Umar (Abu Ubaidah) was quickly named successor, but Godane's death removed the figure most responsible for transforming Al-Shabaab into an Al-Qaeda affiliate capable of international attacks.

events.title: Garissa University Massacre — 148 Students Killed in Kenya
events.detail: On April 2, 2015, Al-Shabaab gunmen attacked Garissa University College in northeastern Kenya, killing 148 students and wounding 79 others. Attackers separated Muslims from Christians and executed Christian students, holding the campus for 15 hours before being killed by Kenyan security forces. It was Kenya's worst terrorist attack since the 1998 US Embassy bombing. The attack was again framed as retaliation for KDF presence in Somalia.

events.title: Wave of Hotel and Government Attacks in Mogadishu
events.detail: Al-Shabaab intensified attacks on Mogadishu targets including the Sahafi Hotel (November 2015, 20+ killed), Central Hotel (February 2015, 25+ killed), and multiple attacks on government checkpoints and convoys. The group demonstrated persistent capacity to penetrate the capital's security perimeter using vehicle-borne IEDs and suicide vests despite significant territorial losses elsewhere.

events.title: Trump Administration Designates Somalia a Zone of Active Hostilities
events.detail: The Trump administration designated parts of Somalia a 'zone of active hostilities' in March 2017, significantly loosening rules of engagement for US military operations. This authorization enabled a dramatic escalation in airstrikes: the US conducted 35 strikes in 2018, up from 14 in 2017 and 3 in 2016. Approximately 150 US Special Operations Forces were deployed to advise Somali forces.

events.title: Zoobe Junction Truck Bombing — Somalia's Deadliest Attack (587+ Killed)
events.detail: On October 14, 2017, a truck loaded with military-grade explosives detonated at the heavily trafficked Zoobe Junction in central Mogadishu, killing at least 587 people and wounding over 300 more—the deadliest single attack in Somalia's history. Al-Shabaab did not immediately claim responsibility, though the attack bore its hallmarks. The bombing destroyed multiple buildings and left a crater 10 meters deep. International condemnation was universal; Turkey flew in medical assistance.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Overruns KDF Base at El Adde — Hundreds Killed
events.detail: On January 15, 2016, Al-Shabaab launched a coordinated dawn assault on a Kenyan Defence Forces (KDF) AMISOM base at El Adde near the Kenyan border. Using vehicle bombs to breach the perimeter, fighters killed an estimated 140–180 Kenyan soldiers—Kenya's worst military casualty event since independence. Kenya refused to confirm the full death toll. Al-Shabaab released video of the assault and claimed to have captured heavy weapons.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Attacks US/Kenya Manda Bay Base — 3 Americans Killed
events.detail: On January 5, 2020, Al-Shabaab fighters breached the Manda Bay Airfield in Kenya, used by US forces to support Somalia operations. Three Americans were killed—one US Army soldier and two DoD contractors—and aircraft were destroyed. It was the most significant American casualty event in the region in years and triggered renewed US airstrikes against Al-Shabaab targets in Somalia.

events.title: Trump Orders Withdrawal of ~700 US Troops from Somalia
events.detail: In December 2020, President Trump ordered the withdrawal of approximately 700 US Special Operations and support personnel from Somalia by January 15, 2021. The Pentagon confirmed most troops relocated to neighboring Kenya and Djibouti, with the stated ability to conduct raids into Somalia. Critics argued the withdrawal weakened AMISOM coordination and emboldened Al-Shabaab. President Biden reversed course in May 2022, redeploying ~450 US troops to Somalia.

events.title: Hassan Sheikh Mohamud Re-elected, Declares Total War on Al-Shabaab
events.detail: Hassan Sheikh Mohamud was re-elected as President of Somalia on May 15, 2022, defeating incumbent Mohamed Abdullahi Mohamed 'Farmaajo.' Within weeks, he declared 'total war' against Al-Shabaab, launching the largest Somali government offensive in years. The declaration was accompanied by a new clan-based mobilization strategy, enlisting Macawisley (clan militia) fighters alongside SNA units.

events.title: AMISOM Transitions to ATMIS (African Transition Mission in Somalia)
events.detail: On April 1, 2022, the African Union Mission in Somalia (AMISOM) transitioned to the African Transition Mission in Somalia (ATMIS), per UNSC Resolution 2628. ATMIS retained the same troop ceiling of 19,626 but shifted its mandate toward capacity transfer to Somali security forces and a phased drawdown. The transition was designed to align with the FGS's goal of full security handover by December 2024.

events.title: SNA & Macawisley Offensive Clears Hiiraan Region
events.detail: Combining SNA forces with popular clan militia (Macawisley) fighters, the Somali government launched its most significant territorial offensive in years across Hiiraan and central regions in August–December 2022. The operation drove Al-Shabaab from dozens of villages, including Eel Jaalle, Mahas, and areas around Beledweyne. The Macawisley mobilization demonstrated effective community-level resistance but required careful clan management to sustain.

events.title: ATMIS Phase 1 Drawdown: 2,000 Troops Withdrawn
events.detail: Per UNSC Resolution 2670, ATMIS completed Phase 1 of its drawdown by June 30, 2023, withdrawing approximately 2,000 troops. Twelve forward operating bases were transferred to Somali security forces. Critics including UNSOM warned the transfer was premature given gaps in SNA readiness. Al-Shabaab launched counter-offensives in Hiiraan shortly after the drawdown, recapturing several villages.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Attacks Education Ministry, Mogadishu — 21 Killed
events.detail: Al-Shabaab fighters attacked the Ministry of Education in Mogadishu on August 28, 2023, detonating a car bomb at the gate before gunmen stormed the building. At least 21 people were killed and dozens more wounded. The Somali government building was hosting officials and job applicants. Security forces secured the building after several hours. The attack underscored Al-Shabaab's continued ability to penetrate Mogadishu's Green Zone perimeter.

events.title: ATMIS Phase 2 Drawdown: Further Troop Reduction
events.detail: ATMIS completed Phase 2 of its drawdown by November 30, 2023, withdrawing another 3,000 troops and transferring additional forward operating bases to the SNA. The drawdown proceeded despite concerns raised by UNSOM about the SNA's capacity to fill the security vacuum. Al-Shabaab exploited gaps in coverage in Bay, Bakool, and Lower Shabelle regions.

events.title: National Consultative Council Convenes — Federal-State Security Compact
events.detail: Somalia's National Consultative Council (NCC), bringing together President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud and all five Federal Member State presidents, convened in July 2023 to align on security strategy, ATMIS drawdown management, and the transition to Somali-led security. The meeting produced agreements on SNA integration of clan militias and resource allocation for front-line states—a rare moment of federal-regional coordination in Somalia's often fractious political landscape.

events.title: ATMIS Mission Ends; AUSSOM Launches with Reduced Ceiling
events.detail: The ATMIS mandate expired December 31, 2024, completing a multi-year drawdown from peak AMISOM strength of ~22,000 troops. The successor African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia (AUSSOM) launched January 1, 2025, with a ceiling of 12,626 troops. Somalia controversially excluded Ethiopia from AUSSOM following the Ethiopia–Somaliland MOU dispute. Uganda subsequently threatened withdrawal after 20 Ugandan peacekeepers were killed in a suicide vehicle bomb. AUSSOM launched underfunded by hundreds of millions of dollars.

events.title: Somali Pirates Hijack MV Ruen — Piracy Resurgence Declared
events.detail: Somali pirates hijacked the Malta-flagged bulk carrier MV Ruen in the Indian Ocean on November 14, 2023—the first successful hijacking of a large commercial vessel since 2017. The incident signaled a concerning resurgence of Somali piracy after over a decade of suppression. Pirates subsequently used MV Ruen as a 'mother ship' for further attacks until Indian Navy INS Kolkata retook the vessel in March 2024.

sources.name: Maritime Security Centre - Horn of Africa

events.title: Indian Navy Commando Raid Retakes MV Ruen
events.detail: Indian Navy's INS Kolkata and maritime patrol aircraft conducted a successful commando operation to retake the hijacked MV Ruen on March 16, 2024. All 17 crew members were freed and 35 Somali pirates surrendered to Indian naval forces. The operation demonstrated the viability of assertive naval responses to piracy resurgence and was widely praised by international maritime community.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Attacks Jazeera Palace Hotel, Mogadishu
events.detail: Al-Shabaab gunmen attacked the Jazeera Palace Hotel—a frequent venue for government meetings and international guests—in Mogadishu in January 2024. The attack involved a vehicle bomb followed by a gunfight. Security forces killed all attackers after several hours. Multiple people were killed and wounded including government officials. The attack reiterated Al-Shabaab's targeting of venues associated with Somalia's political class and foreign presence.

events.title: IMF/World Bank Approve Somalia's HIPC Completion — $4.7B Debt Cancelled
events.detail: On December 13, 2023, the IMF and World Bank approved Somalia's Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC) Completion Point, cancelling approximately $4.7 billion in external debt and reducing Somalia's debt burden from $5.3 billion (2018) to $0.6 billion. The milestone—more than 30 years in the making—restored Somalia's access to international financial markets and multilateral development lending for the first time since the collapse of the Somali state in 1991.

events.title: Lido Beach Restaurant Massacre — 37 Civilians Killed in Mogadishu
events.detail: Al-Shabaab gunmen stormed a popular beachside restaurant at Lido Beach, Mogadishu on August 2, 2024, killing 37 civilians and wounding over 200 during a busy Friday evening. The attack targeted families and young people at one of the capital's most frequented public spaces. The Mogadishu beach area had increasingly become a symbol of the city's tentative normalization; the attack was a deliberate blow against that narrative.

events.title: SNA Offensive Targets Al-Shabaab in Middle Shabelle
events.detail: Somali National Army forces, supported by US AFRICOM airstrikes, launched sustained operations against Al-Shabaab in Middle Shabelle and Lower Shabelle regions throughout 2024. Operations aimed to secure supply routes to Mogadishu and disrupt Al-Shabaab's taxation networks. The SNA claimed liberation of multiple villages but Al-Shabaab demonstrated ability to retake positions through ambush and guerrilla tactics.

events.title: Israel Formally Recognizes Somaliland — First UN-Member Recognition
events.detail: Israel formally recognized Somaliland's independence on December 26, 2025, becoming the first UN member state to do so since Somaliland declared independence in 1991. The recognition was framed in geopolitical terms—Israel sought a Red Sea/Horn of Africa foothold—and was accompanied by Somaliland granting Israel access to Berbera port. The FGS condemned the move as a violation of Somalia's sovereignty. The African Union and Arab League maintained their support for Somalia's territorial integrity, but the recognition marked a watershed in Somaliland's three-decade bid for international legitimacy.

events.title: Al-Shabaab Launches Most Ambitious Offensive Since 2011
events.detail: Al-Shabaab launched its most ambitious territorial campaign since 2011 beginning February–March 2025, conducting coordinated attacks on Jowhar and Adan Yabal (Middle Shabelle), Afgooye (Lower Shabelle), and Bulo Burde (Hiiraan). The group re-linked its central and southern support zones for the first time since the 2022 SNA offensive, reversing most government territorial gains. By July 2025, Al-Shabaab captured Moqokori, Tardo (Hirshabelle), and Mahaas (Hiiraan), and overran an SNA base in Bay region. An assassination attempt via IED targeted President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud near Villa Somalia in March 2025. AUSSOM, underfunded and without Ethiopia, struggled to fill security gaps. Al-Shabaab strength estimated at 7,000–12,000 fighters per the UN Monitoring Group.
